# Team Sizing & Availability Modeling: Research Findings
# Generated By: Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)

## TL;DR

AI tools are reshaping team sizing and capacity planning in software development. Key findings: (1) optimal team sizes are shrinking from 6-10 to 2-5 humans augmented by AI; (2) individual productivity gains of 20-50% do NOT automatically translate to organizational throughput improvements; (3) Brooks's Law still applies -- and is arguably strengthened -- when AI agents are treated as autonomous teammates; (4) the real bottleneck is not code generation (only 25-35% of SDLC) but review, integration, and deployment; (5) sprint velocity recalibration should use conservative multipliers (1.1x-1.3x) rather than the headline productivity numbers.

---

## Source 1: Faros AI -- "The AI Productivity Paradox Research Report" (2025)

- **URL:** https://www.faros.ai/blog/ai-software-engineering
- **Additional URL:** https://www.faros.ai/ai-productivity-paradox
- **Author/Organization:** Faros AI
- **Publication Year:** 2025
- **Data Quality:** High -- based on telemetry from 10,000+ developers across 1,255 teams using source control, task trackers, and CI pipeline data.

### Key Data Points

| Metric | Finding |
|--------|---------|
| Tasks completed (high AI adoption teams) | +21% |
| Pull requests merged (high AI adoption teams) | +98% |
| Tasks touched per day | +9% |
| PRs touched per day | +47% |
| Average PR size increase | +154% |
| Bugs per developer increase | +9% |
| PR review time increase | +91% |
| Company-level DORA metric improvement | No significant correlation |

### Key Findings

- "While individual productivity is soaring, overall throughput -- meaning the rate at which secure, stable, production-ready code is deployed -- is stagnating or even declining in many cases."
- "Despite team-level changes, no significant correlation was observed between AI adoption and improvements at the company level. Across overall throughput, DORA metrics, and quality KPIs, the gains observed in team behavior do not scale when aggregated."
- The report invokes Amdahl's Law: the slowest part of the pipeline dictates overall speed.
- Adoption skews toward less tenured engineers. Lower adoption among senior engineers may signal skepticism about AI's ability to support more complex tasks.
- Most developers use only autocomplete features. Advanced capabilities like chat, context-aware review, or agentic task execution remain largely untapped.
- Six independent research efforts converge on roughly the same ceiling: about 10% productivity gains at the system level.

### Relevance to Team Sizing & Availability

The paradox directly impacts capacity planning: individual developer capacity increases do not translate 1:1 to team throughput. Review bottlenecks absorb the gains. Teams must account for increased review burden when modeling AI-augmented capacity.

---

## Source 2: METR -- "Measuring the Impact of Early-2025 AI on Experienced Open-Source Developer Productivity" (July 2025)

- **URL:** https://metr.org/blog/2025-07-10-early-2025-ai-experienced-os-dev-study/
- **ArXiv:** https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.09089
- **Authors:** Joel Becker, Nate Rush, Elizabeth Barnes, David Rein
- **Publication Year:** 2025
- **Data Quality:** High -- randomized controlled trial (RCT), though small sample size (16 developers, 246 tasks).

### Key Data Points

| Metric | Finding |
|--------|---------|
| Sample size | 16 developers, 246 tasks |
| Developer experience | Average 5 years, 1,500 commits per repo |
| AI tools used | Cursor Pro with Claude 3.5/3.7 Sonnet |
| Actual time change with AI | +19% slower (CI: +2% to +39%) |
| Developer pre-task forecast | Expected AI to reduce time by 24% |
| Developer post-task belief | Believed AI reduced time by 20% |
| AI generation acceptance rate | Less than 44% |
| Participants who saw improvement | 25% |
| Participants who saw degradation | 75% |

### Key Findings

- "When developers were allowed to use AI tools, they took 19% longer to complete issues -- a significant slowdown that goes against developer beliefs and expert forecasts."
- Developers expected AI to speed them up by 24%, and even after experiencing the slowdown, they still believed AI had sped them up by 20%.
- Screen-recording data showed AI-assisted coding had more idle time, suggesting coding with AI requires less cognitive effort and makes it easier to multi-task or zone out.
- AI struggles in large, complex, mature codebases. It often delivers less value to experienced developers working on familiar projects.

### Follow-Up Study (August 2025 onward)

- For the subset of original developers in the later study, estimated speedup shifted to -18% (CI: -38% to +9%) -- suggesting improvement with newer tools but still uncertain.
- Selection bias concern: developers increasingly refused to participate without AI access.

### Relevance to Team Sizing & Availability

This is a critical counterpoint to headline productivity claims. For experienced developers working on familiar codebases, AI may provide zero or negative benefit. Capacity models should NOT assume uniform productivity gains across all developers and all task types.

---

## Source 3: DORA Report 2024 (Google Cloud)

- **URL:** https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/devops-sre/announcing-the-2024-dora-report
- **Author/Organization:** Google DORA Team
- **Publication Year:** 2024 (with 2025 follow-up)
- **Data Quality:** High -- large-scale annual survey (~3,000 respondents).

### Key Data Points

| Metric | Finding |
|--------|---------|
| Respondents using AI for work | 75.9% |
| Delivery throughput change per 25% AI adoption increase | -1.5% |
| Delivery stability change per 25% AI adoption increase | -7.2% |
| Code quality improvement | +3.4% |
| Code review speed improvement | +3.1% |
| Documentation quality improvement | +7.5% |
| Organizational performance increase | +2.3% |
| Team performance increase | +1.4% |
| Respondents with low/no trust in AI code | 39% |

### Key Findings

- "If AI adoption increases by 25%, estimated throughput delivery is expected to decrease by 1.5%, and delivery stability is expected to decrease by 7.2%."
- Individual productivity gains do not translate to system-level delivery improvements.
- Batch size increases when AI is used in coding, leading to riskier, larger changesets.
- 2025 follow-up: AI's relationship with throughput shifted from negative to positive, but AI's correlation with increased instability remains.

### Relevance to Team Sizing & Availability

Sprint capacity planning must account for the stability penalty. Even if velocity increases, the change failure rate may increase, requiring more rework capacity allocation.

## Source 4: Copilot Productivity Study -- Microsoft/MIT/Princeton/Wharton (2024)

- **URL:** https://economics.mit.edu/sites/default/files/inline-files/draft_copilot_experiments.pdf
- **GitHub Blog:** https://github.blog/news-insights/research/research-quantifying-github-copilots-impact-in-the-enterprise-with-accenture/
- **Authors:** Kevin Zheyuan Cui, Mert Demirer, Sonia Jaffe, Leon Musolff, Sida Peng, Tobias Salz
- **Publication Year:** 2024
- **Data Quality:** Medium -- three RCTs with 4,867 total developers, but individually underpowered.

### Key Data Points

| Metric | Finding |
|--------|---------|
| Total participants | 4,867 developers |
| Companies | Microsoft, Accenture, anonymous Fortune 100 |
| Task completion increase (combined) | +26.08% (SE: 10.3%) |
| Commits increase | +13.55% |
| Builds increase | +38.38% |
| Impact on build success rates | No negative impact |
| AI model used | GPT-3.5 |
| Study period | 2022-2023 |

### Caveats

- "Both experiments were poorly powered. At Microsoft, there was low initial uptake of Copilot, and at Accenture, internal organizational changes required analyzing only a random subset."
- Only the PR increase at Microsoft was individually statistically significant.
- Junior/short-tenured developers experienced the most productivity gain.
- Effectively turns "an 8-hour workday into 10 hours of output."

## Source 5: McKinsey -- "Unleashing Developer Productivity with Generative AI" (June 2023)

- **URL:** https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/tech-and-ai/our-insights/unleashing-developer-productivity-with-generative-ai
- **Author/Organization:** McKinsey & Company
- **Publication Year:** 2023
- **Data Quality:** Medium -- controlled lab study with 40+ developers, not a field study.

### Key Data Points

| Metric | Finding |
|--------|---------|
| Sample size | 40+ developers (US and Asia) |
| Task speed improvement | Up to 2x faster |
| Annual spending impact | 20-45% of software engineering function |
| Multi-tool improvement | 1.5-2.5x additional improvement |
| Developer satisfaction | 2x more likely to report happiness and flow |

### Key Findings

- Developers complete coding tasks (generation, refactoring, documentation) 20-50% faster with AI.
- Using two AI tools simultaneously yields 1.5-2.5x additional improvement over one tool.
- Tools better suited for simple prompts than complex multi-framework tasks.

### 2025 Update

- 90%+ of software teams use AI for core engineering tasks.
- Average time savings: 6 hours per week.

## Source 6: Bain & Company -- Technology Report 2025

- **URL:** https://www.bain.com/insights/from-pilots-to-payoff-generative-ai-in-software-development-technology-report-2025/
- **Author/Organization:** Bain & Company (Roy Singh, global head of Advanced Analytics)
- **Publication Year:** 2025
- **Data Quality:** High -- industry-wide analysis.

### Key Data Points

| Metric | Finding |
|--------|---------|
| Coding + testing share of SDLC | 25-35% |
| Productivity boost from basic AI assistants | 10-15% |
| Productivity boost with end-to-end transformation | 25-30% |
| Developer adoption of rolled-out AI tools | Low |

### Key Findings

- "Writing and testing code only accounts for about 25% to 35% of the time from initial idea to product launch. Speeding up these steps does little to reduce time to market if others remain bottlenecked."
- "Teams using AI assistants see 10% to 15% productivity boosts, but often the time saved isn't redirected toward higher-value work."
- "When implemented properly, generative AI could result in efficiency gains of 30% or more" -- Roy Singh
- Real gains require applying AI across the full lifecycle, not just code generation.

### Relevance to Team Sizing & Availability

This is critical for capacity planning: if coding is only 25-35% of the SDLC, even a 50% speedup in coding translates to only 12.5-17.5% overall time savings at best. Capacity models must account for the full lifecycle.

## Source 7: Anthropic -- "Estimating AI Productivity Gains" (November 2025)

- **URL:** https://www.anthropic.com/research/estimating-productivity-gains
- **Author/Organization:** Anthropic
- **Publication Year:** November 2025
- **Data Quality:** Medium-High -- 100,000 conversation transcripts analyzed, but time estimates generated by Claude itself (self-referential methodology).

### Key Data Points

| Metric | Finding |
|--------|---------|
| Conversations analyzed | 100,000 |
| Task-level time reduction | ~80% |
| Potential annual US labor productivity growth increase | +1.8% |
| Total factor productivity increase | +1.1% per year |
| Software developers' share of total AI labor productivity gain | 19% (highest of any occupation) |
| Speedup factor (high school-level tasks) | 9x |
| Speedup factor (college-level tasks) | 12x |

### Internal Anthropic Study

| Metric | Finding |
|--------|---------|
| Engineers surveyed | 132 |
| Qualitative interviews | 53 |
| Usage transcripts analyzed | 200,000 |
| Self-reported productivity increase | 50% |
| AI usage in daily work | 59% |
| Work that wouldn't have been done without AI | 27% |
| Merged PRs per engineer per day increase | +67% |

### Caveats

- Cannot account for additional time spent validating AI output outside conversations.
- Relies on Claude estimating its own time savings (conflict of interest in methodology).
- Not a prediction -- does not account for adoption rates.

## Source 8: California Management Review -- "Seven Myths about AI and Productivity" (October 2025)

- **URL:** https://cmr.berkeley.edu/2025/10/seven-myths-about-ai-and-productivity-what-the-evidence-really-says/
- **Authors:** Dario Gruda, Boram Aeon
- **Publication Year:** October 2025
- **Data Quality:** High -- meta-analytic review of multiple studies.

### Key Findings

- "AI's productivity gains are highly context-dependent, varying significantly by user skill level and task complexity."
- "Human-AI collaboration often underperforms either agent working independently, except in creative tasks."
- "Meta-analytic evidence finds no robust relationship between AI adoption and aggregate productivity gains."
- A 2025 meta-analysis across 83 diagnostic-AI studies: generative models match non-expert clinicians but still trail experts.
- RCT with 5,000+ agents at a US tech support desk: 35% throughput lift for bottom-quartile reps, almost no gain for veterans.

### Relevance to Team Sizing & Availability

AI is a "targeted accelerant" not a "blanket productivity enhancer." Capacity adjustments must be differentiated by developer skill level and task type.

---

## Source 9: Peter Forret -- "The Mythical Agent-Month: Brooks's Law in the Age of Agentic Software Development" (October 2025)

- **URL:** https://blog.forret.com/2025/2025-10-26/mythical-agent-month/
- **Author:** Peter Forret
- **Publication Year:** October 2025
- **Data Quality:** Analysis/opinion piece building on Brooks's original work.

### Key Framework: AI as Tool vs. AI as Teammate

**AI as Tool (e.g., Copilot):**
- Not a new team member -- a "force multiplier"
- Inverts Brooks's Law by reducing communication overhead
- Enables smaller, more agile teams

**AI as Autonomous Teammate (agents):**
- Falls under Brooks's Law -- it IS a new "teammate"
- Costs are deceptively hidden:
  - **Context Engineering** (replaces "ramp-up"): Senior developers must "onboard" the AI
  - **Review Overhead**: AI code is "syntactically perfect but logically flawed"
  - **Debugging Overhead**: "Black box" code with no human mental model of construction

### Brooks's Surgical Team Model Applied to AI

Brooks's "surgical team" maps well onto agentic workflows:
- Chief programmer (human or lead agent) defining structure
- Specialist agents for tests, documentation, migrations
- Single integrator for merging and verification
- "This model scales. Unconstrained parallelism does not."

### Key Quote

"Brooks's Law is not only upheld in the age of autonomous agents; it is strengthened."

---

## Source 10: Stack Overflow -- "Beyond Code Generation: How AI Is Changing Tech Teams' Dynamics" (October 2025)

- **URL:** https://stackoverflow.blog/2025/10/06/beyond-code-generation-how-ai-is-changing-tech-teams-dynamics/
- **Related:** https://stackoverflow.blog/2025/09/11/how-ai-is-reshaping-developer-teams/
- **Author/Organization:** Stack Overflow Blog
- **Publication Year:** October 2025 / September 2025
- **Data Quality:** Journalistic reporting with industry interviews and survey data.

### Key Data Points

| Metric | Finding |
|--------|---------|
| Developers using AI weekly (Q1 2025) | 82% |
| Running 3+ AI tools in parallel | 59% |
| Google code that is AI-assisted | 25% |
| Developers reviewing AI code before merge | 75% |

### Team Structure Changes

- **Google reorganization:** "Teams that used to be organized around 30 to 60 people to deliver a single capability or a single service are starting to break down into smaller components." -- Ryan J. Salva, Google
- "2-3 developers can now build what once required 10+" -- pattern of "concentrated capability teams"
- New role emerging: "AI Integration Lead" for evaluating tools, running pilots, making recommendations
- Engineering Manager role "getting stretched too thin" with AI oversight added

### METR Agent Capability Data

- As of August 2025, leading AI models could complete 2 hours and 17 minutes of continuous work with ~50% confidence of producing a correct answer.
- Task length capability doubling approximately every 7 months.

### Organizational Health Matters

- "For some companies that have a really healthy system, AI has amplified that system -- fewer incidents, moving faster, with higher quality."
- Organizations that were "dysfunctional already are now dysfunctional faster."

## Source 11: OpenAI -- "Building an AI-Native Engineering Team" (2025)

- **URL:** https://developers.openai.com/codex/guides/build-ai-native-engineering-team
- **Related:** https://newsletter.eng-leadership.com/p/how-openais-codex-team-works-and
- **Author/Organization:** OpenAI
- **Publication Year:** 2025
- **Data Quality:** First-party documentation of OpenAI's internal practices.

### Codex Team Structure

| Role | Count |
|------|-------|
| Total team size | ~40 people |
| Product Managers | 1 |
| Designers | 2 |
| Engineers | Remainder (~37) |

### Key Structural Insights

- The Codex team operates with "an unusually high level of autonomy, speed, and trust."
- Multiple small sub-teams work on different projects (open-source agent, IDE extensions, Codex app).
- "Closer to a modern version of Bell Labs" with empowered individual decision-making.

### Multi-Agent Orchestration Roles

OpenAI defines these orchestrated agent roles:
- **Project Manager agent:** Creates requirements, coordinates hand-offs, enforces guardrails
- **Designer, Frontend Developer, Server Developer, Tester agents:** Scoped instructions and output folders
- PM agent enforces gating logic between specialized agents

### The Orchestrator Model

- "The primary job of their engineering team became enabling the agents to do useful work."
- Breaking larger goals into smaller building blocks, prompting agents, using outputs to unlock more complex tasks.
- "Developers are now orchestrating multiple agents across projects: delegating work, running tasks in parallel."

## Source 12: AKF Partners -- "Engineering Team Sizes Are Evolving Rapidly with Agentic AI" (September 2025)

- **URL:** https://akfpartners.com/growth-blog/engineering-team-sizes-are-evolving-rapidly-with-agentic-AI-platforms-the-limits-challenges-and-principles-we-must-consider
- **Author/Organization:** AKF Partners
- **Publication Year:** September 2025
- **Data Quality:** Expert analysis from scaling consultancy.

### Team Size Evolution

| Context | Traditional Size | AI-Augmented Size |
|---------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Two-pizza team | 6-10 humans | 2-3 humans + AI agents |
| Cross-functional unit | 6-10 humans | 4-5 humans + AI (recommended) |
| MVP/single feature | 5-8 humans | 2-3 humans + AI |
| Complex/legacy systems | 8-12+ humans | 4-6 humans + AI |

### Key Findings

- "A single human developer paired with an AI agent could produce code at the pace of a small team."
- AI-driven testing reduces need for dedicated QA engineers.
- AI agents can track progress, allocate tasks, analyze feedback, reducing need for dedicated scrum master or data analyst.
- Practical team composition: "a lead developer, a product manager, and a designer could leverage AI agents to cover coding, testing, deployment, and analytics."
- "Slightly larger teams (4-6 humans) may be optimal for most scenarios" due to complex projects, legacy code, and need for human creativity.

## Source 15: Sprint Capacity Planning -- Multiple Sources

### Velocity Adjustment Formula (from Scrum.org / agile community)

- **URL:** https://www.scrum.org/forum/scrum-forum/85515/sprint-historical-velocity-planning-and-capacity-calculation
- **Data Quality:** Community/practitioner knowledge.

**Key Formula:**
```
Adjusted Velocity = Current Velocity x (New Team Size / Current Team Size)^0.6
```
The 0.6 power factor accounts for the non-linear relationship between team size and productivity (derived from cross-industry data analysis).

### Focus Factor Ranges

| Team Profile | Focus Factor |
|-------------|-------------|
| Experienced, stable, minimal interruptions | 80-90% |
| Typical team with regular meetings | 70-80% |
| High support responsibilities / many meetings | 60-70% |

### Sprint Planning Best Practices

- Plan at 75-85% of the average of the last 3 sprints.
- Stop adding tasks at 80-90% of average velocity to leave buffer.
- Teams lose ~10% of sprint capacity to admin work alone.
- Onboarding new members: expect 50-70% of normal velocity during ramp-up.
- AI-assisted estimation reduces planning meetings from 90 minutes to under 60.
- AI-driven planning improves sprint commitment completion from 60-70% to 85-90%.

## Synthesis: Answers to Specific Research Questions

### 1. How Do AI Tools Change Optimal Team Size?

**Finding: Smaller teams with more output per person.**

| Source | Traditional Size | AI-Augmented Size |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------|
| AKF Partners | 6-10 | 2-3 (MVP), 4-6 (complex) |
| Google (via Stack Overflow) | 30-60 per capability | Smaller components |
| Stack Overflow | 10+ | 2-3 "concentrated capability teams" |
| Pragmatic Engineer (Orosz) | 6-8 specialists | 3-4 full-stack engineers |
| OpenAI Codex Team | N/A | ~40 total, many small sub-teams |

The consensus is that AI enables smaller human teams (2-5 people) to produce what previously required 6-10+. However, 4-6 humans remains the recommended size for complex/enterprise work.

### 2. Does Brooks's Law Still Apply?

**Finding: Yes, and it may be strengthened.**

- When AI is used as a **tool** (Copilot-style): Brooks's Law is partially circumvented. Communication overhead decreases because fewer humans are needed.
- When AI is used as an **autonomous agent**: Brooks's Law applies fully. Context engineering, review overhead, and debugging "black box" code create the same ramp-up and coordination costs.
- "Brooks's Law is not only upheld in the age of autonomous agents; it is strengthened." -- Peter Forret
- Brooks's Surgical Team model maps well to agentic workflows: one human architect + specialist AI agents.

### 3. Individual Capacity Adjustments for AI-Augmented Developers

**Finding: Context-dependent, 0x to 2x, with a realistic central estimate of 1.1x-1.3x at the system level.**

| Source | Individual Gain | System-Level Gain |
|--------|----------------|-------------------|
| Copilot RCT (MIT/Microsoft) | +26% tasks | Not measured |
| McKinsey lab study | +20-50% speed | Not measured |
| Faros AI (field data) | +21% tasks, +98% PRs | ~0% (no DORA improvement) |
| METR RCT (experienced devs) | -19% (slower) | N/A |
| DORA 2024 | Individual flow up | -1.5% throughput, -7.2% stability |
| Bain 2025 (field) | +10-15% | +10% system-level ceiling |
| CMR meta-analysis | Varies by skill | No aggregate gains found |
| Anthropic internal | +50% self-reported | +67% PRs/day |

**Recommended capacity adjustment factors:**
- Junior developers on routine tasks: 1.3x-1.5x
- Mid-level developers on mixed tasks: 1.1x-1.3x
- Senior developers on familiar codebases: 1.0x (no adjustment)
- Any developer on unfamiliar/greenfield work: 1.2x-1.4x

### 4. Sprint Velocity Recalibration with AI Tools

**Finding: Use conservative multipliers and recalibrate frequently.**

- Do NOT apply headline numbers (26%, 50%) directly to sprint velocity.
- Start with 1.1x multiplier on historical velocity and adjust based on observed data.
- Recalibrate quarterly or after major tool/process changes.
- Account for increased review burden (review time +91% per Faros data).
- Plan at 75-85% of adjusted velocity to leave buffer.
- Use formula: `Adjusted Velocity = Historical Velocity x AI_Factor x Focus_Factor x Availability%`
  - Where AI_Factor = 1.1 to 1.3 (conservative) based on team profile
  - Focus_Factor = 0.6 to 0.9 depending on interruptions
  - Availability% = standard PTO/meeting adjustments

### 5. Ratio of "Orchestrators" to "Traditional Developers"

**Finding: Emerging but not yet quantified with hard data.**

- OpenAI's model: engineers shift from "writing code" to "designing environments, specifying intent, and building feedback loops."
- The developer becomes a "supervisor of AI agents" defining goals and constraints.
- No published ratio data exists yet. The closest analog is OpenAI's Codex team: 1 PM : 2 designers : ~37 engineers, all acting as orchestrators.
- AKF Partners suggests: 1 lead developer + 1 PM + 1 designer as minimum viable human team, with AI agents handling execution.

### 6. Availability Modeling with AI Context Switching

**Finding: No specific published models exist, but several principles emerge.**

- AI-assisted coding shows more idle time (METR study), suggesting context-switching patterns differ.
- Developers running 3+ AI tools in parallel (59% per Stack Overflow) need different availability assumptions.
- Traditional focus factors (70-80%) may need adjustment downward (60-70%) to account for AI tool management overhead, or upward (80-90%) if AI automates administrative work.
- 27% of AI-assisted work at Anthropic "wouldn't have been done without AI" -- suggesting AI enables NEW work, not just faster existing work.

### 7. Industry Data on Team Restructuring

| Organization | Change |
|-------------|--------|
| Google | 30-60 person teams breaking into smaller components |
| OpenAI | 40-person team with extreme autonomy and small sub-teams |
| Anthropic | 67% more PRs/day after Claude Code adoption |
| Steve Yegge prediction | ~50% workforce reduction at large companies |
| Gergely Orosz prediction | Specialist teams -> 3-4 full-stack engineers |

### 8. Capacity Planning Formulas Accounting for AI

**No standardized formulas exist yet.** Based on the evidence, a proposed framework:

```
Effective_Capacity = Team_Size x Hours_Per_Sprint x Focus_Factor x AI_Multiplier x (1 - Rework_Factor)

Where:
- Focus_Factor: 0.6-0.9 (standard)
- AI_Multiplier: 1.0-1.5 (by role/task profile, see table in #3)
- Rework_Factor: 0.05-0.15 (accounts for AI-introduced bugs and review overhead)
  - Per Faros: +9% bugs, +91% review time
  - Per DORA: -7.2% stability
```

**The Bain Correction:**
Since coding is only 25-35% of SDLC, the effective AI impact on total delivery capacity is:
```
Total_AI_Impact = AI_Coding_Speedup x SDLC_Coding_Share
Example: 1.3x speedup x 0.30 share = 1.09x total impact (9% improvement)
```

This aligns with Bain's field observation of 10-15% system-level gains and the convergence of six independent studies at ~10% system-level ceiling.

---

## Data Quality Summary

| Source | Type | Quality | Key Caveat |
|--------|------|---------|------------|
| Faros AI | Field telemetry, 10K+ devs | High | Correlation not causation |
| METR | RCT | High | Small sample (n=16) |
| DORA 2024 | Survey, ~3K respondents | High | Self-reported adoption levels |
| Copilot RCT | RCT, 4,867 devs | Medium | Underpowered individual experiments |
| McKinsey | Lab study, 40+ devs | Medium | Lab setting, not field conditions |
| Bain 2025 | Industry analysis | High | Aggregate, not granular |
| Anthropic | Internal + conversation analysis | Medium | Self-referential (Claude estimates Claude's value) |
| CMR | Meta-analysis | High | Review of reviews, no new primary data |
| Peter Forret | Analysis piece | Low-Medium | Opinion/framework, not empirical |
| Stack Overflow | Journalistic + interviews | Medium | Selective reporting |
| OpenAI | First-party documentation | Medium | Self-promotional interest |
| AKF Partners | Expert analysis | Medium | Consulting firm perspective |
| Pragmatic Engineer | Expert commentary + survey | Medium | Partially paywalled |
| NetCorp | Aggregated statistics | Low-Medium | Secondary source, attribution unclear |

---
